TA1-income-core:
No — Act 592 is no longer Ghana's governing income-tax statute. It has been superseded by the **Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)**, which now occupies that ground. 

**What Act 592 was, and its trajectory before repeal.** The Internal Revenue Act, 2000 was, on its own terms,an Act "to amend and consolidate the law relating to Income Tax, Capital Gains Tax and Gift Tax and to provide for related matters,"assented to on22nd December, 2000.It was the consolidating income-tax code of its era, and it was itself amended repeatedly over the following years —by the Internal Revenue (Amendment) Act, 2002 (Act 622), the Internal Revenue (Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 644), the 2004 amendment (Act 669), and the two 2006 amendments (Acts 700 and 710),among others. That pattern of serial amendment tells you Act 592 was live and operative through the 2000s.

**The first shift — administration, not substance (2009).** The first thing to keep separate is that Act 592 was *touched* by the Ghana Revenue Authority Act, 2009 (Act 791) without being repealed by it. Act 791 was an institutional reform: an Act"to establish the Ghana Revenue Authority to replace the Internal Revenue Service, the Customs, Excise and Preventive Service and the Value Added Tax Service for the administration of taxes."Its consequential-amendments Schedule reached into Act 592, amending itby substituting "Commissioner-General" for "Commissioner" throughout, and "Ghana Revenue Authority" for "Internal Revenue Service" wherever those terms appeared in the Act and its Regulations.Critically, the enactments Act 791 actually *repealed* were the agency-governance statutes —the Revenue Agencies (Governing) Board Act, 1998 (Act 558); the Internal Revenue Service Act, 1986 (P.N.D.C.L. 143); and the Revenue Agencies (Retention of Part of Revenue) Act, 2002 (Act 628)— not Act 592 itself. So after 2009 the *administrator* of income tax changed, but the charging statute remained Act 592, merely re-badged. This is the diachronic trap: a source showing Act 791 amending Act 592 must not be read as Act 791 replacing it.

**The decisive shift — substantive replacement (2015).** Act 592 was displaced as the substantive income-tax law by the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896). The clearest proof in the materials is that the entire post-2015 stream of income-tax amendments operates on Act 896 as the principal enactment, and never on Act 592. The 2021 rate revision amended"The Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) referred to in this Act as the 'principal enactment'"in its First Schedule; the 2023 amendment likewiseamended "The Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)... by the insertion after section 2, of" a new minimum-chargeable-income section 2A.Parliament, in other words, treats Act 896 — not Act 592 — as the living income-tax code it edits. Confirming this at the highest level, the consolidated schedule of revenue laws under the current framework lists"Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)"as the income-tax statute subject to GRA administration, with no mention of Act 592.

**Why this matters, and the residual life of Act 592.** The current governing statute is Act 896, and every operative income-tax rule — individual rates, withholding, the minimum-chargeable-income charge, sector rebates — should be stated from Act 896 and its amendments, not from Act 592. Act 592 survives, if at all, only for historical periods and as the source of concepts Act 896 re-enacted; it no longer governs a current year of assessment. Applying *lex posterior derogat priori*, a comprehensive re-enactment of the same subject-matter supersedes its predecessor, and Act 896 is exactly that — a fresh income-tax code that Parliament now amends in Act 592's place.

One honest limit on the record in front of me: the materials show Act 896 comprehensively occupying the income-tax field and being serially amended, but the express repeal-and-savings provision of Act 896 (which would formally have repealed Act 592) is not among the retrieved passages. The supersession is unmistakable from how Parliament now legislates, but the precise repealing section should be confirmed at the repeals provision of Act 896 itself.
TA2-income-amend:
Yes — the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) has been amended repeatedly. The materials show it operating as a "principal enactment" that a steady stream of annual amendment Acts has revised, so its current form is Act 896 as modified by each of those later Acts. 

**The Act is a living, repeatedly-amended statute.** Each amending Act expressly names Act 896 as the "principal enactment" and then inserts, substitutes or adds provisions to it. That is the drafting technique that tells you Act 896 itself remains the governing instrument — the amendments do not stand alone but fold into it. For example, the 2023 amendment declares thatthe Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896), referred to in this Act as the "principal enactment", is amended by the insertion after section 2.

Working through the amendments in the materials, in chronological order:

**2021 — Act 1066 (COVID-era relief).** This Act operated on the Sixth Schedule. Its long title records that it amendsthe Sixth Schedule to the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) to provide for a rebate for selected industrial sectors, suspend quarterly instalment payments by specified self-employed persons and owners of commercial vehicles and for related matters. Concretely, it added a rebate paragraph under whichthe rate of income tax payable on the estimated chargeable income of the following sectors for the second, third and fourth quarters of 2021 shall be seventy percent of the applicable rate— covering accommodation and food, education, travel and tours, and arts and entertainment.

**2021 — Act 1071 (rates and thresholds).** Later that year the Act was amended again, this time reaching the First, Second and Sixth Schedules. Its purpose was toreview the rates of income tax for individuals; to reduce the withholding tax rate for sale of unprocessed gold by small scale miners; to increase the threshold for an individual to whom the presumptive tax under the Modified Taxation Scheme applies; to extend the COVID-19 concessions granted in 2021 for further six months in 2022. It substituted a revised individual income-tax band table, running from aFirst GH¢4,380 taxed at Nil, up to income exceeding GH¢240,000 taxed at 30 per cent.

**2023 — Act 1094 (structural changes).** This is the most substantial amendment in the materials. Its long title sets out a broad programme: torevise the rates of income tax for individuals and introduce an additional income tax bracket; introduce a withholding tax rate on the realisation of assets and liabilities and on winnings from lottery; revise the treatment of foreign exchange losses; revise the upper limits for the quantification of motor vehicle benefits and provide for related matters. Its arrangement of sections confirms the reach — it touchessections 2A, 6, 17, 25, 39A, 94, 100A, 115, 116A, 119, 128 and 133, and the First and Fourth Schedulesof Act 896. Among the concrete changes: it introduced a **minimum chargeable income** regime, under whichdespite section 2, a person may be required to compute and pay tax on a minimum chargeable income, calculated in substance asa per cent of turnover where the person has been declaring losses for the previous five years of assessment, but not applying to a person within the first five years of commencement of operations or engaged in farming. It broughtwinnings from lotteryinto section 6, reworked the treatment of foreign-exchange losses in section 25 — providing, for instance, thatan unrealised foreign exchange loss shall not be allowed as a deduction, and a foreign exchange loss arising from a transaction between two resident persons shall not be allowed as a deduction— and inserted a new section 39A requiring thata person who realises an asset or a liability shall, within thirty days after the realisation, submit to the Commissioner-General a return in the form determined by the Commissioner-General.

**A point on how these interact.** Because each Act amends the same schedules and sections, the later instrument controls where they overlap. The individual rate table is the clearest illustration: Act 1071 substituted the First Schedule table in late 2021, but Act 1094 in 2023 again revised the individual rates and added a further bracket — so the current individual-rate structure is the Act 1094 version, not the 1071 one. Reading the 1071 band table as still live would misstate the law; it explains where the rates had reached by end-2021, but the 2023 revision supersedes it.

**So, in current form**, Act 896 is the standing income-tax code as amended by (at least) Acts 1066, 1071 and 1094, incorporating the minimum-chargeable-income rule, the additional individual bracket, the withholding charges on asset realisations and lottery winnings, and the revised foreign-exchange-loss treatment. The revenue-administration side of the same regime has also moved: the Revenue Administration Act, 2016 (Act 915) — which governs returns and appeals for Act 896 — was itself amended to, among other things,establish an Independent Tax Appeals Board [and] provide for the payment of a shortfall in tax liability without payment of a penalty, and to align its Second Schedule with the new section 39A return.

Two honest caveats on precision. First, several of these texts are OCR extractions flagged for verification against the official published versions, so the exact figures (band amounts, turnover percentage) should be confirmed against the gazetted Acts before you rely on them. Second, Ghana's income-tax law is amended almost every fiscal year; the amendments above are those in front of me, and there may be further amendment Acts after 2023 not captured here — the safest statement is that Act 896 remains the principal enactment and must be read together with every subsequent amending Act to establish its state on any given date.
TA3-vat-new:
The governing VAT statute in Ghana today is the **Value Added Tax Act, 2025 (Act 1151)** — not Act 870. This is the single most important point, and it changes how you must read everything else in the file.

**Why Act 1151 now governs, and what it did to Act 870**

For roughly a decade the foundational instrument was Act 870, introduced, as the abridged version records,to revise and consolidate the law relating to the imposition of the value added tax (VAT) and to provide for related matters. That was its constitutional character — a consolidating statute, meant to be the one place the VAT law lived.

Act 1151 does to Act 870 exactly what Act 870 did to *its* predecessor: it re-enacts the whole field. Its long title is almost word-for-word the consolidating formula —an Act to revise and consolidate the law relating to the imposition of the Value Added Tax and to provide for related matters. When a later statute "revises and consolidates" the same ground its predecessor occupied, the ordinary operation of *lex posterior derogat priori*, reinforced here by an express repeal, means the earlier Act is superseded. Act 1151 confirms this on its own face: its arrangement of sections contains a dedicated provision —"73. Repeals and savings"— alongsidetransitional provisions and a commencement provision. That is the machinery by which Act 870 is repealed and its subject matter carried into the new consolidation. Act 1151received assent on 9th December, 2025.

The imposition of tax now rests on Act 1151, not Act 870. The charging provision reads:there is imposed by this Act, a Value Added Tax to be charged on the supply of goods or services made in the country, other than exempt goods or services; and the import of goods or services, other than exempt import.It further provides thatunless otherwise provided in this Act, the Tax shall be charged on the supply of goods or services where the supply is a taxable supply and made by a taxable person in the course of the taxable activity of that person.Structurally the same architecture as Act 870 — but the operative words now derive their validity from the 2025 Act.

**How the law travelled from 2013 to 2025 — the chain of amendments**

The reason so much of your file predates Act 1151 is that Act 870 was not static; it was amended almost every year, and those amendments are the story of how the law reached its present shape. Tracing them shows the *direction of travel* the 2025 consolidation ultimately captured:

- **2017 (Act 954)** removed VAT on lotto stakes and introduced VAT withholding — itremoved the tax on stakes in the National Lotto and provided for the withholding from the payment of Value Added Tax to registered VAT traders, inserting the withholding-agent machinery (new sections 47A/47B and the section 52 return obligations).
- **2021 (Act 1072)** narrowed the flat rate, confining it toa retailer with a turnover not exceeding five hundred thousand Ghana Cedis per annum, and extended the textiles zero-rate.
- **2022 was the pivotal year.** Act 1082 brought VAT into the digital and cross-border economy —up-front payment of VAT by an unregistered importer, the taxation of electronic commerce, the electronic issuance of a tax invoice, and the zero-rating of locally assembled vehicles. Then Act 1087 delivered the headline rate change:an increase in the Value Added Tax rate, removal of the tax exemption for imported textbooks and newspapers, exemption of betting and other games of chance, and revision of the transitional provisions and administrative penalty for non-implementation of the electronic VAT system. Concretely, the standard rate moved tofifteen percent, calculated on the value of the taxable supply of the goods or services, or the value of the import.
- **2023 (Act 1107)** added flat-rate treatment for commercial-property rentals and estate developers, extended various zero-rates andintroduced a zero tax rate for locally manufactured sanitary towels and waived the tax on electric vehicles for public transportation.

A caution on reading these amendments today. Because they operated *on Act 870*, and Act 870 is now repealed, they are no longer live as free-standing instruments — their content survives only insofar as Act 1151 re-enacts it. So the correct method is: state the current rule from Act 1151, and use the 2013–2023 amendment history only to explain *why* a provision takes the shape it now does. Note too that the abridged Act 870 in your file still shows the old figure —the rate of the tax is twelve and a half percent (12.5%)— which was overtaken by the 15% rate in Act 1087 and is now carried forward under Act 1151. Treat that 12.5% as spent; it measures the distance travelled, not the law in force.

**One important qualifier that sits outside the VAT Act itself**

VAT exemptions are no longer governed solely by the VAT statute. The **Exemptions Act, 2022 (Act 1083)** was enactedto regulate the application of tax exemptions and other exemptions and to provide for related matters, and it applies broadly —this Act applies to all exemptions. Critically, it contains a paramountcy clause:where there is a conflict or inconsistency between the provisions of this Act and any other enactment related to exemptions, the provisions of this Act shall prevail.So even under Act 1151, the exemption and zero-rating schedules must be read subject to Act 1083. The VAT Act is also administered within the wider GRA architecture — theValue Added Tax Act, 2013 (Act 870)appears in the list of revenue laws subject to full administration by the Ghana Revenue Authority; that listing will be read as a reference to the current VAT statute now consolidating that field.

**Bottom line**

The law in force today is Act 1151 of 2025, which revised and consolidated the entire VAT regime and repealed Act 870. Act 870 and its string of amendments (Acts 954, 1072, 1082, 1087 and 1107) are best understood as the evolutionary record that produced the current consolidation — historically essential for interpreting particular provisions, but no longer the operative law. When you need the exact wording of a specific current rule — for instance the precise standard rate, the flat-rate threshold, or the repeal-and-savings mechanics — confirm it at the relevant section of Act 1151 itself (the charging provision at section 1, and the repeals/savings and transitional provisions at sections 73–74).
TA4-vat-repeal-detail:
The short answer is that Act 870 no longer governs. The Value Added Tax Act, 2025 (Act 1151) is the VAT statute now in force, and it operates as the comprehensive re-enactment that supersedes the 2013 Act. Your trader is bound by Act 1151, not Act 870.

**Why the 2025 Act displaces the 2013 Act**

The mechanism is the ordinary one of *lex posterior derogat priori*: where the legislature re-enacts a whole field, the later comprehensive statute takes over from its predecessor. Act 1151 is exactly that kind of instrument — a full VAT code carrying its own imposition, registration, supply, invoicing, assessment and offence provisions. You can see the completeness across the retrieved text: it defines the"supply of goods" to mean an arrangement under which an owner parts with possession by way of sale, barter, lease, transfer, exchange, gift or similar disposition, or a supply of power, heat, refrigeration or ventilation, and excludes the supply of money; it sets out its own cancellation-of-registration regime, under whichthe Commissioner-General shall cancel the registration of a taxable person where satisfied that the person no longer exists, is not carrying on a taxable activity, is not required or entitled to register, has not kept proper accounting records, or has not submitted regular and reliable returns; and it contains its own assessment power triggered where the Commissioner-General has reason to believe, for instance, thata taxable person fails to submit a tax return, a return is incorrect, or lawful Tax has not been paid. A statute that re-legislates the entire subject-matter in this way leaves nothing for the earlier Act to govern.

That is the same technique Act 870 itself used in 2013. Act 870 was **"an Act to revise and consolidate the law relating to the imposition of the value added tax"**, and when it came into force it expressly swept away the previous VAT regime —its repeal and savings section provided that a list of earlier enactments, beginning with the Value Added Tax Act, 1998 (Act 546) and its several amendment Acts, were repealed. Act 1151, bearing the later title *Value Added Tax Act, 2025*, does to Act 870 precisely what Act 870 did to Act 546: a fresh consolidation ordinarily carries an equivalent repeal of the statute it replaces.

**A necessary caveat on the pinpoint**

I should be candid about the limit of what the retrieved passages show. The provisions of Act 1151 in front of me confirm that it is a self-standing, comprehensive VAT code now in force, but the specific repeal-and-savings section of Act 1151 — the provision that would expressly repeal Act 870 and preserve accrued rights — is not among the passages I can quote here. The conclusion that Act 870 is repealed rests on the nature of Act 1151 as a complete re-enactment and on the settled interpretive rule, not on a repeal clause I have read. The one targeted step to close this is to confirm the express repeal-and-savings provision in Act 1151 itself, which will also tell you the treatment of anything straddling the changeover.

**The trajectory tells the same story**

Reading the regime diachronically reinforces the point rather than complicating it. Act 870 was not a static text; it was repeatedly amended — the 2017 (No. 2) Act removing VAT on National Lotto stakes and introducing withholding,"to remove the tax on stakes in the National Lotto, to provide for the withholding from the payment of Value Added Tax to registered Value Added Tax traders"; the 2021 Act confining the flat rate,"to limit the application of the flat rate to a retailer with a turnover not exceeding five hundred thousand Ghana Cedis per annum"; the 2022 Act bringing in up-front payment by unregistered importers, e-commerce taxation and electronic invoicing; and the 2023 Act reworking flat rates on rental and estate-developer supplies and extending various zero ratings. Each of these amended Act 870 in place. Act 1151 is a different kind of instrument altogether — not another amendment to the 2013 principal enactment, but a new principal enactment. That is the line the law has now crossed.

**One point of continuity worth flagging**

Even under Act 1151, VAT does not sit in isolation.The Revenue Administration Act, 2016 (Act 915) is to be read as one with each of the other tax laws, andthe powers of the Commissioner-General under Act 915 may be exercised with respect to any tax law, in addition to specific powers granted under other tax laws. So your trader's administrative obligations — assessments, appeals, recovery — continue to be shaped by Act 915 operating alongside whichever VAT statute is current; the change from Act 870 to Act 1151 does not disturb that overlay.

**Bottom line**

Advise the trader that its VAT rights and obligations are now governed by the Value Added Tax Act, 2025 (Act 1151). Act 870 ceased to apply because Act 1151 is a comprehensive re-enactment of the VAT law that supersedes it, on the same consolidate-and-replace basis by which Act 870 replaced Act 546 in 2013. The only outstanding confirmation is the exact repeal-and-savings provision in Act 1151, which should be checked to fix the precise cut-off date and the treatment of any transactions or returns spanning the transition.
TB1-592-employment:
The provision that imposes income tax on employment (personal-emolument) income is best understood as working through several linked sections of Act 592 rather than a single stand-alone "employment tax" charging section.

The charging provision itself is **section 1**.Section 1 provides that a person who has a chargeable income shall pay, subject to this Act, for each year of assessment income tax as calculated in accordance with this Act.The tax is then quantified by reference to the rate schedule:the income tax payable for a year of assessment is calculated by applying the rates of tax under the relevant Part of the First Schedule to the chargeable income of that person for the year, and from the resulting amount there is subtracted any tax credits allowed to that person for the year.Employment income enters the charge because "chargeable income" is built up from assessable income.Under section 5, the chargeable income of a person for a year of assessment is the total of that person's assessable income for the year from each business, employment, and investment, less the total amount of deductions allowed under sections 13 to 22, 39, 57 and 60.Assessable income, in turn, is dealt with in Part III:under section 6, subject to the Act, the assessable income of a person for a year of assessment is from any business, employment, or investment.The specific head that brings **employment (personal-emolument) income** into the assessment is **section 8 — "Income from an Employment"**, which the Act's arrangement of sections lists as the employment source within the assessable-income Division.Section 8 is headed "Income from an Employment".So the accurate answer is that Act 592 imposes tax on employment income through the interaction of **section 1** (the imposition/charge), **section 8** (which characterises income from an employment as assessable income), **section 6** (assessable income), and **section 5** (chargeable income), with the rate applied under the First Schedule via section 1(2). If you need the exact operative wording of section 8 itself — the definition of what falls within employment income — that text sits at section 8 of the Act and should be pinpointed there; the passages before me confirm its existence and heading but not its full subsection text.

One point of currency worth flagging: Act 592 has since been replaced by the **Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)**, whose framework mirrors this structure.Under Act 896, a person is liable to pay income tax on that person's chargeable income; chargeable income is made up of income from employment, business or investment less any allowable deduction; and assessable income is income of a person from any employment, business or investment.Under that later Act, an individual's employment income is the gains and profits from the employment for the year, including salary, wages, leave pay, fees, commissions, gratuities, overtime pay, bonuses and other benefits and allowances paid in cash or given in kind.If your question concerns a current year of assessment, the live charging provisions are those of Act 896; Act 592's sections 1, 5, 6 and 8 govern periods when it was in force and explain the structure Act 896 carried forward.
TB2-592-rental:
Rental and investment income under Act 592 was charged through the assessable-income machinery in **section 9 ("Income from an Investment")**, read with the Part III framework that treats income from a business, an employment, and an investment as the three sources of assessable income.Part III—Assessable Income lists section 6 (Assessable Income), section 7 (Income from a Business), section 8 (Income from an Employment) and section 9 (Income from an Investment).Section 9 is the operative charging hook for investment income.Section 9(1) provides that a person's income from an investment is that person's gains or profits from any investment.Rent sits squarely within that source:the gains or profits of a person from an investment include any dividends from a non-resident company, interest, charge, annuity, royalties, rent, natural resource payment, or other income accruing to or derived by that person from the investment other than an amount included in ascertaining that person's income from a business or employment.So rental income, unless it forms part of a business, is charged as investment income under section 9.

**What governs it now.** This is the part that matters most, because Act 592 no longer states the current law. The tax on investment and rental income today rests on the **Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)**, which superseded Act 592 as the consolidated income-tax statute. The materials confirm that Act 896 is the enactment in force:the Income Tax (Amendment) Act, 2023 (Act 1094) is "AN ACT to amend the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)."The 2023 amendment treats Act 896 as the live framework —"The Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896)... referred to in this Act as the 'principal enactment', is amended by the insertion after section 2, of 'Minimum chargeable income 2A.'"In practical terms, then, the position has moved as follows: section 9 of Act 592 was the historical charging provision for investment and rental income, but it is spent as live law — Act 592 was replaced by the 2015 consolidation, and the operative source is now Act 896 as amended (including by Act 1094). Two features of the current administration and withholding regime appear directly in the materials:

- **Withholding on investment-type payments.** Under Act 896 as amended,a resident person must withhold tax at the rate specified in paragraph 8 of the First Schedule where that person pays any dividend, winnings from lottery, interest, natural resource payment, rent or royalty to another person, or consideration in respect of the realisation of an asset or a liability, and the payment has a source in the country.Rent is thus caught by the withholding machinery in section 115 of Act 896.
- **Administration.** Assessment, objection and appeal now run through the Revenue Administration Act, 2016 (Act 915), administered by the Commissioner-General."Commissioner-General" means the Commissioner-General appointed under section 13 of the Ghana Revenue Authority Act, 2009 (Act 791).One caution on pinpoints. The exact current charging section for investment income in Act 896 (Act 896 renumbered the Act 592 scheme) is not among the passages in front of me; the retrieved Act 896 material shows the withholding rule at section 115 and the new minimum-chargeable-income rule at section 2A, but not the equivalent of Act 592's section 9. So the precise present-day section number for the charge on investment/rental income should be confirmed against Act 896 itself rather than assumed to remain "section 9."

**Bottom line:** under the old law, investment and rental income was charged under **section 9 of Act 592** (within the Part III assessable-income regime). That Act has been superseded; the charge now lives in the **Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) as amended** (notably by Act 1094), with withholding on rent and other investment payments under section 115 of Act 896 and administration under Act 915 — confirm the exact current charging section against Act 896.
TC1-896-current:
Act 896 remains the principal income tax statute in force. It has not been replaced by any new principal Act — instead, it continues to be operated in the ordinary way, by a succession of amending Acts that graft changes onto the original text while leaving the parent enactment intact.

The materials show this pattern unmistakably. Every income-tax amending Act in the collection treats Act 896 as the living principal enactment it modifies, never as something superseded. The Income Tax (Amendment) Act, 2021 (Act 1066) works by amendingthe Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) in the Sixth Schedule by the addition after paragraph 12of a new paragraph. Its companion later that year, Act 1071, describes itself asan Act to amend the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) to review the rates of income tax for individualsand related matters — again amending, not repealing.

The most recent income-tax amendment in the materials confirms the position continues into 2023. Act 1094refers to the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) as the "principal enactment", and proceeds to amend it — for example byamending the principal enactment in section 6 by the addition after subparagraph (vi) of paragraph (a) of subsection (2), of "(vii) winnings from lottery", and byinserting after section 39, a new section 39A on submission of returns for gains on realisation of assets and liabilities. You cannot insert a section 39A into a repealed Act; the very drafting technique presupposes that Act 896's section numbering is still live law.

This is the ordinary operation of the *lex posterior* principle at the level of individual provisions rather than whole statutes. Each amending Act displaces only the specific subsection or schedule it substitutes — Act 1094 substituting a new subsection (1) of section 17, for instance (the principal enactment is amended in section 17 by the substitution for subsection (1)) — while everything it does not touch continues in force as originally enacted. The correct reading of the law as it now stands is therefore a consolidated one: Act 896 as amended by Acts 1066, 1071, 1094 and the others, read together.

Two further features of the materials reinforce that Act 896 is treated as the settled backbone of the wider revenue architecture. First, sister statutes cross-refer to it as current law: the Revenue Administration Act, 2016 (Act 915) defines the income-tax returns for its own purposes asa return of income filed under section 124 of the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) and a statement of tax withheld filed under section 117 of Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896). Second, the interest and penalty machinery in Act 915 is expressly pegged to Act 896 —section 70 applies where an estimate of tax payable under section 122 of the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) is less than ninety percent of the correct amount. A statute repealed and replaced would not still be supplying the operative reference points for the revenue-administration regime as of the 2022 amendments.

A candid limit worth stating: currency is a fact, and I am reasoning from amendments up to Act 1094 (2023). On the strength of these materials, the better view — indeed the plainly correct one — is that Act 896 is in force as the principal Income Tax Act, amended but not replaced; nothing in the collection is a new principal Income Tax Act, and every income-tax instrument here is styled and drafted as an amendment to Act 896. If you need to be certain the position has not shifted after the most recent instrument in front of me, the single confirming step is to check for any post-2023 principal Act or consolidation, but the materials themselves point firmly to continuity.
TC2-1151-current:
The Value Added Tax Act, 2025 (Act 1151) is the current, governing VAT statute in Ghana, and nothing in the materials shows it has since been amended or repealed. 

Act 1151 is a consolidating statute —an Act "to revise and consolidate the law relating to the imposition of the Value Added Tax and to provide for related matters,"assented to on9th December, 2025.A comprehensive re-enactment of this kind is not merely "also present" alongside the older law; by operation of *lex posterior derogat priori* it displaces what came before. And Act 1151 does exactly that expressly. Its repeals-and-savings provision sweeps away the whole prior lineage — the principal 2013 Act (Act 870) and the string of amendments that had been bolted onto it over the years, includingthe Value Added Tax (Amendment) Act, 2022 (Act 1082), the Value Added Tax (Amendment) (No. 2) Act, 2022 (Act 1087), the Value Added Tax (Amendment) Act, 2023 (Act 1107), and the Value Added Tax (Amendment) Act, 2025 (Act 1133).This is decisive for how you treat the earlier documents in the file. The 2013 Act (Act 870) and every amendment listed in section 73 — Acts 954, 1072, 1082, 1087, 1094 (income tax aside), 1107 and 1133 — are spent as operative law. They explain how the present rules took shape, but they no longer *bind*. The live rule on any point is what Act 1151 now provides. So, for instance, the 12.5% standard rate and 3% flat rate you find described in the abridged Act 870 material are historical reference points, not the current charge; the operative charging provisions are now those in section 1 and following of Act 1151, andthe tax is charged on the supply of goods or services made in the country other than exempt supplies, and on the import of goods or services other than exempt imports, where the supply is a taxable supply made by a taxable person in the course of taxable activity.Two features of Act 1151 confirm both its currency and its careful management of the transition — and they matter practically.

First, commencement. Assent in December 2025 does not equal force.The Act comes into force on the 1st day of January, 2026.There is therefore a short window in which assent has occurred but the repeals have not yet bitten, and the Act legislates for it:the enactments repealed under section 73 continue to apply for the years of assessment commencing prior to the date on which this Act comes into force.So the old regime still governs pre-2026 assessment years even though it is repealed going forward — a standard savings mechanism that prevents a gap.

Second, continuity of subordinate law. Repealing the parent Acts does not automatically kill the regulations and administrative acts made under them.Regulations, byelaws, notices, orders, directions, appointments or any other act lawfully made under the repealed enactments, which are not inconsistent with this Act and were in force immediately before commencement, are treated as made under this Act and continue in effect until reviewed, cancelled or terminated.Likewise,the repeal does not affect the operation of offences committed, penalties imposed or proceedings commenced before the Act comes into force.Accrued liabilities and live prosecutions therefore survive under the old law.

So the answer, stated plainly: Act 1151 is the current VAT law. On the materials before us there is no later VAT Act amending or repealing it — the most recent instrument in the chain, the 2025 amendment (Act 1133), is one of the enactments Act 1151 *itself* repeals, which tells you the consolidation was drafted to absorb and supersede everything up to and including 2025. Two qualifications keep the earlier Acts in play at the margins: they still govern pre-2026 assessment years, and offences, penalties and proceedings that had already crystallised before 1 January 2026 remain governed by the law as it stood.

One practical caution on method rather than substance: legislative currency is a moving target, and a VAT statute is amended frequently in Ghana. The point I can stand behind from these materials is that nothing here post-dates or disturbs Act 1151; if you need to rely on its currency as at a date after 1 January 2026, the single confirming step is to check the Parliament of Ghana gazette for any VAT (Amendment) Act enacted after Act 1151 came into force.

The **Value Added Tax (Amendment) Act, 2025 (Act 1133)** appears in the collection only as a *name* in a repeals list. The new consolidating statute — the Value Added Tax Act, 2025 (Act 1151) — lists among the enactments it repealsthe Value Added Tax (Amendment) Act, 2025 (Act 1133).That tells us Act 1133 existed and has now been swept up into the consolidation, but it tells us nothing about the operative content of Act 1133 — which section of the principal Act it amended, or what substantive change it made to the VAT regime.

None of the passages here reproduce the arrangement of sections or the amending provisions of Act 1133 itself. So I cannot honestly cite you a section of Act 1133 or state what it altered without inventing it — and a fabricated pinpoint or a guessed-at change is worse than a candid gap. I won't manufacture one.

A few things I *can* say with confidence, which may be what you actually need:

**Act 1133 is now spent.** Whatever it did to the old regime, it amended the Value Added Tax Act, 2013 (Act 870), and both Act 870 and Act 1133 have since been repealed and replaced by the 2025 consolidation. Act 1151 is"AN ACT to revise and consolidate the law relating to the imposition of the Value Added Tax and to provide for related matters,"and it takes effect on a clean date:this Act shall come into force on the 1st day of January, 2026.The reason Act 1133's substance no longer governs is that a comprehensive re-enactment supersedes the amending Acts that fed into its predecessor — so for the law *as it now stands*, the live text is Act 1151, and Act 1133 matters only as legislative history.

That said, the repeal is not total.Despite the repeal, the Regulations, byelaws, notices, orders, directions, appointments or any other act lawfully made under the repealed enactments which are not inconsistent with this Act and in force immediately before commencement shall be considered to have been made under this Act and shall continue to have effect until reviewed, cancelled or terminated.And for past tax years,the enactments repealed under section 73 shall continue to apply for the years of assessment commencing prior to the date on which this Act comes into force.So if your question concerns a pre-2026 assessment year, whatever Act 1133 changed *does* still bite for that period — which is exactly why its content would be worth pinning down.

To get the specific amending provision and the change it made, the one targeted step is to pull the arrangement-of-sections and marginal notes of Act 1133 itself (its long title will state its purpose in a single sentence, as every other amendment in this series does). That is the document that carries the answer; it simply isn't among the passages retrieved for this query.
